data.title: Dynex Series C: On My Watchlist, But The Price Level Isn't Right Just Yet
data.description:
This article discusses Dynex Capital, Inc. 6.9% SER C PFD (DX.PR.C), which is a preferred share issuance, by Dynex Capital (DX), a mortgage REIT whose ordinary shares I've applauded for quite some time. I think we could witness a mix-up in credit risk quite soon, which would likely see investors move up the capital structure. Dynex's Series C provides a solid 'up-the-capital-structure' candidate, but I think it remains a hold for now, until it re-prices lower.

Here's why Dynex's Series C is on my watchlist and why I'm waiting for a prudent buying opportunity.

Dynex's Recent Performance Update

Dynex's Series C has gained around 2.25% in year-over-year price return. In addition, its going-in yield of 6.90% expanded after March 15, as the issuance reached its variable-rate date; it now pays three-month LIBOR plus 5.461%, possessing a current yield of ~9.50%.

From a company-specific perspective, we've seen Dynex perform relatively well lately. The firm delivered net profitability in 2025, reflected by its net income per common share of $2.49. In addition, Dynex maintained a clean balance sheet, shown by its book value per common share of $13.45 (per 31 December, 2025). Minor disregards were evident after the company missed its revenue target by $15.92 million in Q4. However, overall, a year-over-year common stock total return of ~24.23%, marked confidence, if we consider that mREITs can be sensitive to value destruction via their high payout structures.

Series C's Headline Variables

Let's isolate Series C (Figure 3). As mentioned before, the issuance recently breached its fixed-to-floating date (and call date). The news wasn't overly discouraging to investors, as LIBOR (or SOFR) remains quite high. The SOFR is at around 3.65%, and although inflation has cooled, we remain a long distance away from benchmark rates reaching a level that would drag Dynex Series C's current yield below its initial 6.90%. Even if nominal yields fell to such an extent, we would still see real yields hold up, as inflation would almost certainly have moved lower in lockstep (unless rates shifted down on the basis of a growth scare).

Additional features of Series C include that it pays quarterly, it's cumulative, and, of course, it currently trades at a premium-to-liquidation value.

Commentary

Leveraged RMBS Market Outlook

I'll run through a few company-specific factors later. However, I'd first like to start by discussing the leveraged residential mortgage-backed securities environment.

We've seen mortgage rates hold steady and Treasury yields rise, during the past year, providing spread support to RMBS. Simultaneously, lower short-term benchmark rates have supported a reduction in borrowing costs, further assisted by loosening of financial conditions and compressed credit spreads.

While I think benchmark rates will continue to drift lower, we are seeing some cracks in the mortgage market, revealed by figure 5, which shows delinquency rates rising. Moreover, I am worried about credit spreads being sub-300-350 (high yield) for as long as they have been. Credit spreads have a strong mean-reverting component, partially due to the economy's cyclical short-run properties. Do I think these risks warrant divestment from RMBS? No, I just think they deserve some attention, especially if leveraged RMBS vehicles are the topic of discussion (leveraged RMBS can be volatile).

Company-Specific Factors

As previously mentioned, Dynex's company-specific factors are stable. As seen in figure 6, the company delivered $308.88 million in net income to common stockholders during fiscal 2025, even after covering obligations to Series C shareholders. Notably, the asset-liability relationship was well-managed, reflected by a net interest margin of ~21.43%. Based on my market commentary in the previous section, I would highlight that NII seems sustainable. I'd just be cognizant of the fact that Dynex's $258 million in net gains on investments might not be sustainable throughout the cycle (gains/losses will happen cyclically).

Figure 7 shows the company's balance sheet. The total assets/total liabilities ratio is 1.17x (author's calculations), showing the firm's distance-to-default. The ratio is quite sound in my view; Dynex's business model operates on a lot of debt, given its leveraged RMBS strategy. I would've been worried if, say, a mature consumer cyclical company's distance-to-default was near 1x, but not Dynex's. In addition, shareholders have shown faith in the company's balance sheet lately, illustrated by its $1.2 billion in capital raises achieved during 2025.

I discussed Dynex's portfolio on numerous occasions last year when I covered its common shares. The portfolio's fair value has increased by ~5.2 billion since I last covered it. The exposure has stayed more or less the same; Dynex remains focused on the 4.5%–5.5% coupon range in Agency RMBS pools. Its to-be-announced exposure is notable at 16%, which creates some variability in going-in rates, but overall, the fund's asset-liability relationship is stable, given its weighted-average funding rate of 4.10% (this figure is based on repo funding and not cost of preferred shares or external debt).

Why the Preferred Stock?

So, why am I watching the preferred shares when the common stock dividend yields at 14.6% and shares in business growth? I'm watching the preferred shares because I think leveraged RMBS funds might eventually face a credit event or two in the next year. Financial conditions have been easy for a while, credit spreads are arguably complacent, and leveraged RMBS isn't a secular momentum trade (it's a mean-reversion trade). In other words, I'm still positive about Dynex's common stock, but I'm waiting for a market shock to occur, which would likely send both the common and preferred shares down. However, the difference would be: Investors will likely move up the capital structure in a rebound due to the preferred's set liquidation value and coupon, while the commons could languish for a sustained period (see this link and notice how long the commons can trend down) - I'm essentially thinking about my move, in the event that the market's risk appetite normalises.

I would wait for Dynex's Series C to trade below its liquidation value before engaging. The risk-return distribution is concave at this stage; prices have arguably reached a ceiling. I'll wait for it to trade at a material discount before reassessing, allowing me to add convexity to the price risk-return distribution, while capturing a higher going-in dividend yield. Of course, it should be noted that returns aren't guaranteed; all companies carry insolvency risk, especially during credit events.

Conclusion

I'm keeping Dynex Capital, Inc. 6.9% SER C PFD on my watchlist, as I believe investors will shift up the capital structure in due course. However, the issuance is trading slightly rich, partially spurred on after its reset date in March 2025; I'll be waiting for a market-based credit event or cyclical normalization from Dynex before considering Series C.

As for Dynex's underlying operations, the company remains robust with a solid distance-to-default, confidence from investors, a good net interest margin, and selective positioning in pooled RMBS. That being said, cyclical unrealized gain and a supportive credit environment have allowed results to sustain momentum; I won't be surprised if cyclical normalization occurs at some stage.

data.title: Tesco: Overvalued Based On Low Margins, Competitive Environment, And Regulatory Headwinds
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tesco (TSCDY) is a ubiquitous brand in the British Isles, operating over four thousand stores across the U.K. and Ireland. The firm has consistently gained market share in recent years and pays a ~3% dividend, with the benefit of zero withholding tax on this income for U.S. owners.

Unfortunately, though, while shares look superficially cheap on the basis of operating earnings and free cash flow, I think it is important to balance this against long-term risks to the firm's low margins, especially as a result of the potential for increased labour costs in the coming years.

Overall, also taking into consideration the highly competitive, commoditized nature of the U.K. grocery business, I rate Tesco a sell overall. Recent results have been strong, but based on historical results in tougher market conditions, I believe that there is a high likelihood of future margin compression.

Recent Performance

Tesco shares have performed very well in dollar terms over the past three years, with substantial price appreciation in the underlying shares, which trade in London, and a slight boost from the weakening of the dollar versus the pound.

Adding in dividends, as shown above, investors who bought Tesco three years ago have achieved a very impressive 150% total return, and given the overall quality of the business, it is fair to say that it was relatively undervalued back in 2023. Given that the stock is up so significantly, however, I believe that the risk-reward profile for owning Tesco has now shifted in the other direction.

Superficial Positives

On the surface, it's very easy to present a bullish case for Tesco stock, especially when one looks at the extent to which it is gaining market share in the U.K. Based on "total till roll" statistics (i.e. comparing how much money passed through cash registers for different supermarkets) shown above in Tesco's interim 2025-2026 results, Tesco's market share in the U.K. rose by 0.8 percentage points between 2024 and 2025, which looks like strong growth for a (very) mature and established brand over such a short period.

However, I am concerned by the fact that equivalent growth is not visible in the Republic of Ireland statistics, which is fundamentally a very similar market to the U.K. from the perspective of the grocery business. Despite market share growth of an entire percentage point between 2023 and 2024, in Ireland, the equivalent growth for the most recent period was just 0.1 percentage points, a very significant slowdown in growth compared to the prior year-on-year growth of one percentage point. Considering also the extent to which price increases can follow through (positively) into total till roll while having a long-term negative effect on customer retention, I do think some caution is warranted around these superficially positive numbers.

Valuation

The other main argument that might contribute to a buy case for Tesco is the fact that it trades at a relatively low multiple of operating earnings. Dividing the TTM operating earnings of around ~$4 billion into the firm's $41.5 billion USD market cap, we come to a multiple of around 10.2x, which is relatively low versus the broad market and may lead some to view Tesco as a "value" play.

However, it's worth considering Tesco's valuation in the context of its U.K. grocery industry peers. J Sainsbury's (JSNSF) is perhaps the closest peer, being the second largest supermarket operator in Britain. The fact that Sainsbury's trades at a TTM operating income multiple of just 7.4x (versus 10.2x from Tesco) illustrates quite clearly the premium being applied to Tesco by the market, though based on my concerns, this premium is not warranted.

Also, taking Tesco's recent guidance on free cash flow for the full financial year, we come to a much less appealing ratio on this basis. The firm expects "£1.4bn to £1.8bn" in FCF for FY25-26, which would give us a price-to-FCF multiple of between 16.9x and 21.8x, which is far from compelling. Again, Sainsbury's seems the better value pick here, with a 13.2x price-to-FCF multiple for the last twelve months.

Also, I think it's important to view these numbers in the context of a high probability of future margin compression for Tesco and the broad supermarket sector, which I will discuss in a moment, making the "value" case much less credible.

Wage Pressure

Even if we take market share data at face value, I think investors should remember that, while a strong brand is an important aspect of running a supermarket business, the commoditized and consequently highly competitive nature of the industry means that there always tends to be pressure on margins as operators compete on price. For Tesco, this is illustrated by the fact that operating margins reached a level as low as 1.5% in the 2016 financial year, during a period of particularly intense competition with the European "discounter" supermarkets, Aldi and Lidl.

Taking price competition to one side, the big risk to Tesco's margins from my perspective comes from the potential for increased labour costs. The U.K. has relatively robust minimum wage laws, and Tesco typically currently offers £12.64 per hour versus the current minimum of £12.21 (with the statutory minimum increasing to £12.71 from April). While this is generally a relatively predictable annual cost that increases broadly in line with inflation, I do think investors should pay attention to the political context of the U.K., which may result in upward pressure on the minimum wage.

Currently, the Labour government led by Prime Minister Keir Starmer is very unpopular, with recent polls suggesting that as few as 16% of the public will vote for the party at the next election. This has led to calls for the Prime Minister to be replaced by somebody further left of the party, with candidates such as Angela Rayner, the former Deputy Prime Minister, a potential candidate. She has advanced policies, including a Workers' Rights Bill, which will likely increase costs for large employers such as Tesco. And more broadly, even the current centre-left government has significantly increased payroll taxes (National Insurance) for employers from 13.8% to 15%. So overall, the political climate is not particularly favorable to margin expansion for Tesco and may even result in margin compression.

Upside Catalysts

Clearly, it is important to illustrate the other side to the "labor" issue for Tesco. In my view, the main risk to the upside for Tesco is in relation to structural improvements in the supermarket sector, which would reduce labor costs, currently one of the biggest costs for the broad industry.

As shown above, Tesco is making significant investments in ensuring that its supply chain is not so labor-intensive, as demonstrated by the opening of the semi-automated 621,000 sq ft food distribution centre in Aylesford recently.

The other factor to be aware of in terms of labor costs is the potential for a right-wing government to be elected at the next scheduled election in 2029, which would be much less likely to pursue significant increases to the minimum wage, with Reform UK (currently polling around 29%) leader Nigel Farage recently proposing a reduction in the minimum wage for young people.

In the near term, a stabilization of the current U.K. government would be a positive factor, as it would remove the risk of left-wing policies (with the potential to damage Tesco) being introduced.

In addition, any indication of broad strengthening of the U.K. consumer (versus just minimum wage workers) would be more bullish for Tesco, with real wage growth tending to translate into more spending on higher-margin products. Higher consumer confidence and higher employment rates are also likely to boost Tesco's prospects for the same reason, though none of the above seem particularly likely in the short to medium term, given Britain's relatively weak economic growth in recent years.

Yield

While I've presented a bearish case overall, it is still worth highlighting Tesco's dividend yield, which, unlike most foreign stocks, attracts zero withholding tax due to being based in the UK, where no withholding tax regime exists. Its 3% yield is relatively strong versus the ~1% currently available from the S&P 500, though overall I don't think it is worth buying Tesco for its yield based on the risks I've discussed above. It's also worth noting that on a historical basis, 3% is not a very strong deal versus the yield in excess of 6% available when purchasing the stock at a more depressed price level back in 2022.

Conclusion

Tesco is a high-quality brand, but as is the case in many commodity-type sectors, this does not necessarily result in it being a high-quality business from a long-term investment perspective. I am particularly cautious based on very thin margins historically and the potential for political and competitive pressures to weigh on these margins going forward, in spite of relatively strong performance in recent years. Given that Tesco stock is up significantly over the past three years, I think a sell rating is warranted given the quite poor risk-reward profile of the stock at the moment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Artisan Global Equity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investing Environment

Global equity markets delivered mixed but generally positive results in Q4 2025, as investors balanced easing monetary policy against slowing economic growth and elevated geopolitical uncertainty. In the US, concerns surrounding a potential artificial intelligence ('AI') bubble intensified, leading mega-cap technology stocks to slow and, in some cases, stagnate following outsized year-to-date gains. This moderation in leadership, combined with broader economic uncertainty, fueled a market rotation as investors sought less volatile opportunities across other segments of the market. Monetary policy developments also played a key role. The Federal Reserve implemented three rate cuts during the year and signaled the potential for additional easing in 2026. As expectations for a lower rate environment solidified, the US dollar weakened significantly, declining approximately 17% over the course of 2025 and providing a tailwind to international assets.

Outside the US, equity markets reflected similar crosscurrents. European stocks posted positive gains during the quarter, supported by easing financial conditions, though returns were tempered by slowing growth and lingering political uncertainty. Asian markets also advanced, as investors looked past uneven economic momentum and rising geopolitical risk. Emerging markets, by contrast, lagged during the quarter, pressured by shifting capital flows, currency movements and softer global demand.

Geopolitical tensions remained elevated throughout the quarter but had a negligible impact on market growth. While intermittent progress toward a potential peace framework in Ukraine offered moments of optimism, broader risks persisted. China's increasingly assertive posture toward Japan and Taiwan, alongside renewed instability in Latin America, including rising tensions involving Venezuela, added to an already complex global backdrop as markets closed out the year.

Portfolio Performance

In 2025, the Global Equity portfolio outperformed its benchmark, doubling the returns of the MSCI All Country World Index. Our disciplined stock selection was a key contributor to performance. Our conviction in the industrials sector paid off, driving the portfolio's performance with triple-digit returns. Other positive sources of returns came from our holdings in financials, health care, utilities and consumer staples, significantly outperforming benchmark returns in each of these sectors. Stock selection in materials was negative but was partially offset by favorable currency movements. Information technology ('IT') weighed on relative performance due to our underweighting, although absolute returns in the sector were solid.

In Q4, the portfolio underperformed the MSCI All Country World Index. Our stock selection in industrials was the leading cause of relative underperformance. However, stock selection in the utilities, IT and health care sectors helped buoy portfolio performance relative to the index.

Our quarterly performance was largely attributable to holdings in industrials, particularly our defense theme. Two of our top-performing holdings this year, South Korean defense companies Hanwha Aerospace (HNWHF) and LIG Nex1 (LIGNF), declined in October and November. Investor profit-taking and a broader near-term pullback in the defense sector weighed on the shares of both companies, moderating the respective triple- and double-digit YTD gains recorded this year for each stock.

Hanwha Aerospace, the largest defense company in South Korea with significant exposure to naval, land and aero-based systems, continues to expand its global presence in defense spending. Our conviction in the company remains high. Healthy cash flow and order backlog will likely lead to near-term growth. Additional ammunition and land-based-system deals continue to be struck with European governments as they invest to become independently secure outside of NATO security agreements. The Middle East has also emerged as an area of opportunity for Korean defense companies, with specific interest in Hanwha's K9 self-propelled howitzer and Chunmoo rocket system. In Q4, Korean defense names secured $8.5 billion of domestic and export orders.

On a positive note, utilities was an area of strength during the quarter. National Grid (NGG) and SSE (SSEZY) both contributed positively, reflecting investor confidence in regulated asset bases, long-duration cash flows and accelerating investment tied to grid modernization and renewable energy infrastructure. SSE received a strong positive market reaction after announcing a 5-year, £33 billion investment plan focused on upgrading UK electricity networks and expanding its renewables footprint, reinforcing the defensive and growth characteristics of high-quality utilities amid economic uncertainty.

Sticking with our theme of electrification, LS Electric also contributed positively, benefiting from increased global spending on grid modernization and power transmission infrastructure. The manufacturer and distributor of electrical power equipment continued to be supported by strong demand tied to electrification trends and international infrastructure investment, which we believe underpin attractive long-term growth opportunities. The company added significant deals to its pipeline during the period, the most notable being a contract to supply high-voltage transformers worth 460 billion KRW ($312 million) to a renewable power plant that will supply a large data center being developed in the Southeastern US. LS Electric is also expected to benefit from Japan's commitment to invest up to $25 billion in US infrastructure and grid modernization.

UBS Group (UBS) was a meaningful contributor to performance, despite the portfolio's overall financials sector underperformance this quarter. The financial services company's shares advanced to 17-year highs as investors responded positively to indications that Swiss regulators may adopt a more balanced approach to future capital requirements than initially proposed earlier in the year. A more constructive regulatory framework would allow the bank to maintain balance sheet flexibility while supporting capital returns, helping it to maintainits competitive position internationally. Operationally, UBS has continued to execute well, with resilient net new money inflows across its core wealth management franchise and steady progress integrating Credit Suisse. We believe the combination of cost synergies, improving operating efficiency and disciplined capital management will help UBS deliver sustainable earnings growth over the near term, even amid a more uncertain macroeconomic environment in Europe.

Pivoting to the materials sector, the portfolio lagged benchmark performance. As our smallest sector in the portfolio, the impact on performance was negligible. However, it is worth noting that we exited our position in Air Liquide (AIQUY) over the last few months based on valuation. We will continue to monitor the company as we view its strategic positioning in decarbonization and ongoing contract wins supporting durable earnings potential.

Position Activity

This year, we benefited from our investment theses in three macroeconomic themes shaping the world: financials, electrification, and aerospace and defense (what we refer to as FED). Our adjustments during the quarter were modest and primarily reflected valuation considerations and ongoing portfolio risk management, rather than a change in our long-term investment outlook on global trends in the FED themes.

Beginning with electrification, one of our largest adds this quarter was in Samsung Electronics (SSNLF). The position provided exposure to AI infrastructure growth. Samsung has been a direct beneficiary of high demand for AI-related chips, including dynamic random access memory ('DRAM') and high-bandwidth memory ('HBM'). Recently, Samsung Electronics' sixth-generation HBM semiconductor, HBM4, recorded the highest operating speed in Broadcom (AVGO)'s technical tests, underscoring the company's accelerated expansion of HBM market share. Samsung's strong HBM4 performance results boosted bullish sentiment toward semiconductor manufacturers, helping drive chipmaker stocks higher in Q4.

In industrials, we increased our position in MNC Solutions and Contemporary Amperex Technology Co Limited (CIEHY) ('CATL'). MNC provides mission-critical power and automation systems that support defense-related infrastructure and security applications, areas seeing sustained investment amid elevated geopolitical uncertainty. While the shares experienced near-term weakness, we believe the company's exposure to long-duration defense spending and infrastructure modernization remains intact. The company has delivered solid revenue growth, which we believe will grow by ~20% over the next couple of years, supported by its specialized product mix and long-term contracts. In a macro environment characterized by heightened geopolitical risk and increased focus on national security, we view MNC's specialized capabilities and defensive end-market exposure as supportive of its long-term earnings profile.

CATL, on the other hand, is a potential key long-term beneficiary of global electrification. As we mentioned in previous letters, the company is a leading manufacturer and distributor of battery products, particularly within electric vehicles and energy storage systems. While near-term sentiment toward the sector has been volatile amid pricing pressure and slower growth in certain EV markets, we believe CATL's scale, technology leadership and cost advantages differentiate it from competitors. Recent market volatility offered an attractive opportunity to build exposure to a high-quality franchise aligned with long-duration electrification tailwinds.

Alternatively, in industrials, we exited our positions in BAE Systems (BAESY) and RTX Corporation (RTX), following strong performances over the course of the year. We believe valuations for these holdings were fully reflected in their price, leading us to realize gains.

In consumer discretionary, we exited Amazon (AMZN) after the stock rebounded sharply and approached our assessment of fair value. While we remain constructive on Amazon's long-term competitive positioning, we saw more attractive opportunities offering better risk-adjusted return potential elsewhere in the portfolio. We also exited our position in MercadoLibre (MELI), an online trading site for Latin American markets, after modest gains, driven by improving profitability and margin expansion. Given the stock's appreciation, we chose to lock in gains and reallocate capital toward opportunities with more compelling valuations.

As we closed out the year, we began increasing our portfolio weighting in consumer staples from 4% to nearly 9%. While it is not listed as a key theme for the portfolio, we have identified several names in the sector that enhance portfolio balance, while also exhibiting high-quality characteristics, such as strong brands, disciplined capital allocation and pricing power. We believe these attributes are increasingly valuable in a more uncertain macroeconomic environment, as they can provide resilient cash flows and help complement the portfolio's more growth-oriented holdings.

One such name is Tesco (TSCDY), a UK-based multinational grocery and retail company operating a leading supermarket chain. Now one of our top 10 holdings in the portfolio, Tesco has continued to benefit from its scale advantages and price leadership, which have allowed it to gain market share despite a competitive environment and a UK consumer backdrop that remains value conscious. At the same time, margin contributions from initiatives in retail media and continued share repurchases can support earnings growth without requiring an improvement in consumer spending.

Outlook

Looking ahead, we believe the investment backdrop in Europe and international markets more broadly continues to offer a compelling opportunity set, particularly as government spending becomes an increasingly important driver of economic activity. Across much of Europe, governments remain the largest source of incremental demand, with public investment playing a meaningful role in GDP through defense, infrastructure, energy security and grid modernization. Our portfolio themes are closely aligned with these spending priorities, particularly in areas where capital allocation is visible, has long duration and is supported by policy objectives.

Persistently elevated geopolitical tensions have led us to evolve our portfolio positioning in order to maintain a balanced opportunity set. We have been reducing exposure to the defense theme after a year of strong performance and redeploying that capital into electrification-related investments, where we see durable demand driven by grid expansion, energy transition and rising electricity consumption. At the same time, we believe European financials are increasingly well positioned, supported by improved balance sheets, more disciplined capital allocation and a stabilizing macroeconomic environment, even amid ongoing geopolitical uncertainty in the region.

International markets remain an attractive source of value and opportunity relative to the US. In 2025, US equities lagged global peers, with the S&P 500® Index rising 17.8% compared with a 32.4% gain for the MSCI All Country World ex US Index—the widest gap since 2009. Despite this strong performance, international equities continue to trade at a meaningful valuation discount. As of mid-January, the S&P 500® Index was valued at 22.98X forward earnings, while major international markets were generally priced in the teens. Against this backdrop, we continue to favor international equities, where valuations are more reasonable and earnings growth is supported by structural investment trends rather than multiple expansion alone.

Team Update

Andrew Euretig, Associate Portfolio Manager on the Global Equity team, is no longer employed with Artisan Partners. Navdeep Singh is also no longer with the team. Coverage of the stocks Andrew and Navdeep followed have been fully absorbed by other team members, and the team is operating well.

We seek to invest in companies, within our preferred themes, with sustainable growth characteristics at attractive valuations that do not fully reflect their long-term potential.

Themes

We identify long-term secular growth trends with the objective of investing in companies that have meaningful exposure to these trends. Our fundamental analysis focuses on those industry leaders with attractive growth and valuation characteristics that will be long-term beneficiaries of any structural change and/or trend.

Sustainable Growth

We apply a fundamental approach to identifying the long-term, sustainable growth characteristics of potential investments. We seek high-quality companies that typically have a sustainable competitive advantage, a superior business model and a high-quality management team.

Valuation

We use multiple valuation metrics to establish a target price range. We assess the relationship between our estimate of a company's sustainable growth prospects and its current valuation.
